summary: This PR extends `rebuild_auth` to strip the Authorization header when the scheme or port changes on a redirect, in addition to the existing hostname check. The logic is conceptually correct but has a notable bug with implicit vs. explicit default ports that could cause spurious header stripping, and the new port-change branch lacks test coverage. A few style and secondary test-coverage issues are also present.

findings.title: Port-change redirect stripping has no test coverage
findings.detail: The new `or original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch has no corresponding test. The PR adds a test for scheme changes and retains the hostname-change test, but there is no test that redirects to the same scheme and host on a different port and verifies the Authorization header is stripped (positive case), nor one that confirms the header is retained when the port is unchanged (negative case). This leaves a significant portion of the new logic unverified.

findings.title: Port comparison gives false positives for implicit default ports
findings.detail: Python's `urlparse` returns `None` for `.port` when no port is explicitly specified in the URL (e.g. `https://example.com` → `port=None`), but returns the integer `443` when stated explicitly (e.g. `https://example.com:443` → `port=443`). Because `None != 443` evaluates to `True`, a redirect from `https://host/` to `https://host:443/` (or `http://host/` to `http://host:80/`) will incorrectly trigger the port-change branch and strip the Authorization header even though the canonical authority is identical. No credentials are leaked, but users whose servers redirect to a canonically explicit-port form will silently lose authentication on every redirect. The fix is to normalise the port before comparing by substituting the scheme's default port when `.port` is `None`, e.g.: `DEFAULT_PORTS = {'https': 443, 'http': 80}` and then comparing `(DEFAULT_PORTS.get(scheme) if parsed.port is None else parsed.port)` for both sides.

findings.title: netrc lookup may re-introduce credentials after scheme-downgrade strip
findings.detail: After correctly stripping the Authorization header on a scheme downgrade (e.g. https → http), `rebuild_auth` unconditionally queries `.netrc` for the new (potentially http) URL and re-applies any matching credentials. If the user's `.netrc` contains an entry for the http host, credentials will be re-sent in plaintext over the downgraded connection, defeating the purpose of the strip. This is a pre-existing design issue not introduced by this PR, but the PR's expanded stripping logic makes it more likely to be encountered. Consider skipping the netrc lookup when the scheme has been explicitly downgraded (https → http).

findings.title: Scheme-redirect test is integration-only and covers only one direction
findings.detail: The sole coverage for the scheme-change branch is the new integration test `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect`, which requires live `httpbin_secure` and `httpbin` fixtures plus an SSL CA bundle, making it fragile in environments without SSL fixtures. There is no isolated/unit-level test (e.g. using `RedirectSession` or direct `rebuild_auth` calls) that exercises the scheme comparison independently. Additionally, only the https→http downgrade direction is tested; the symmetric http→https case is absent. While the condition is symmetric in the code, an explicit test for the reverse direction would confirm correctness and guard against future asymmetric changes.

findings.title: Missing trailing comma after last keyword argument in new test
findings.detail: Every other multi-line `requests.get(...)` call in the test file ends each keyword argument line with a trailing comma, including the last argument before the closing parenthesis. The new `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` test omits the trailing comma after `verify=httpbin_ca_bundle` (line ~1585), which is inconsistent with the established convention in the file.

findings.title: Dense multi-condition `if` should be extracted into a named variable
findings.detail: The new three-part condition is correct but dense inline. The surrounding codebase style (e.g. `merge_setting`) prefers extracting compound boolean expressions into named variables. Introducing an intermediate variable such as `same_origin = (original_parsed.scheme == redirect_parsed.scheme and original_parsed.hostname == redirect_parsed.hostname and original_parsed.port == redirect_parsed.port)` followed by `if not same_origin:` would improve readability and directly echo the PR description's own "canonical root URL" language.

findings.title: Port comparison may give false positives for implicit default ports
findings.detail: Python's `urllib.parse.urlparse` (and the `urlparse` shim used here) returns `None` for `.port` when no port is explicitly specified in the URL, even if the scheme implies a well-known port. This means that a redirect from `https://example.com` (port=None) to `https://example.com:443` (port=443) — or vice versa — will trigger the `original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch and unnecessarily strip the Authorization header even though the canonical authority is identical. Similarly, a redirect between `http://example.com` (port=None) and `http://example.com:80` (port=80) would incorrectly strip the header. The comparison should normalise the port by falling back to the scheme's default port (80 for http, 443 for https) before comparing, e.g. by using a helper that maps scheme→default port and substitutes it when `.port` is None.

findings.title: Implicit vs. explicit default port allows Authorization header to be incorrectly stripped on same-origin redirect
findings.detail: urlparse returns `None` for the port when it is the scheme's default (e.g. `https://host/` → port=None), but returns the integer `443` when it is stated explicitly (e.g. `https://host:443/`). Because `None != 443` is True, a redirect from `https://host/` to `https://host:443/` (or vice-versa) will trigger unnecessary stripping of the Authorization header. This is a false positive: credentials are not leaked, but users whose servers redirect to the canonical explicit-port form will silently lose authentication on every redirect. The fix is to normalise the port by substituting the default port when the parsed value is None (e.g. using `{'https': 443, 'http': 80}.get(scheme)`).

findings.title: netrc re-applies credentials after scheme-downgrade strip, potentially re-introducing plaintext credential exposure
findings.detail: After correctly stripping the Authorization header on a scheme change (e.g. https → http), rebuild_auth unconditionally queries .netrc for the new (potentially http) URL and re-applies any credentials it finds (line 251-253). If the user's .netrc contains an entry for the http host, credentials will be re-sent in plaintext over the downgraded connection, defeating the purpose of the strip. This is a pre-existing design issue that the PR did not address. Consider skipping the netrc lookup when the scheme has been downgraded (https → http).

findings.title: Multi-condition `if` could use a helper variable for clarity
findings.detail: The existing code used a simple single-condition `if`. The new three-part condition is correct but somewhat dense inline. The surrounding style (e.g. the `merge_setting` function) prefers extracting compound boolean expressions into named variables or helper functions. A small intermediate variable such as `same_origin = (original_parsed.scheme == redirect_parsed.scheme and original_parsed.hostname == redirect_parsed.hostname and original_parsed.port == redirect_parsed.port)` and then `if not same_origin:` would match that style and make the intent immediately clear. The term "same origin" or "same root" also echoes the PR description's own language ("canonical root URL").

findings.title: New test method is missing a trailing comma after `verify=httpbin_ca_bundle`
findings.detail: Every other multi-line `requests.get(...)` call in the test file (e.g. lines 1573-1576 in the same hunk) ends each keyword argument line with a trailing comma, including the last one before the closing parenthesis. The new test omits the trailing comma after `verify=httpbin_ca_bundle` (line 1585), which is inconsistent with that convention.

findings.title: Port-change redirect stripping not covered by any test
findings.detail: The new `or original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch (line 246) has no corresponding test. The PR adds a test for scheme changes (https→http) and keeps the hostname-change test, but there is no test that redirects to the same scheme and host on a different port and verifies the Authorization header is stripped. Both the positive case (header stripped) and negative case (same port → header retained) are absent.

findings.title: No unit-level (mock-based) test for scheme-change stripping to complement integration test
findings.detail: The only coverage for the scheme-change branch is the new integration test `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect`, which requires both `httpbin_secure` and `httpbin` fixtures and an SSL CA bundle. There is no isolated/unit-level test (e.g. using `RedirectSession` or direct `rebuild_auth` calls) exercising the scheme comparison, making the test fragile and leaving the logic uncovered in environments without the SSL fixtures.

findings.title: Scheme redirect test only covers https→http downgrade; http→https upgrade not tested
findings.detail: The new `test_auth_is_stripped_on_scheme_redirect` test redirects from https to http. The symmetric case (http→https) is a different code path in practice and is not exercised. While the condition is symmetric in the code, an explicit test for the http→https direction would confirm correctness for that scenario and is absent.

findings.title: Port-change redirect stripping has no test coverage
findings.detail: The new `or original_parsed.port != redirect_parsed.port` branch has no corresponding test. The PR adds a test for scheme changes and retains the hostname-change test, but there is no test that redirects to the same scheme and host on a different port and verifies the Authorization header is stripped (positive case), nor one that confirms the header is retained when the port is unchanged (negative case). This leaves a significant portion of the new logic unverified.

findings.title: Port comparison gives false positives for implicit default ports
findings.detail: Python's `urlparse` returns `None` for `.port` when no port is explicitly specified in the URL (e.g. `https://example.com` → `port=None`), but returns the integer `443` when stated explicitly (e.g. `https://example.com:443` → `port=443`). Because `None != 443` evaluates to `True`, a redirect from `https://host/` to `https://host:443/` (or `http://host/` to `http://host:80/`) will incorrectly trigger the port-change branch and strip the Authorization header even though the canonical authority is identical. findings.detail: After correctly stripping the Authorization header on a scheme downgrade (e.g. https → http), `rebuild_auth` unconditionally queries `.netrc` for the new (potentially http) URL and re-applies any matching credentials.
